---
title: "3.3 Instance Methods"
subtitle: "Class Creation"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit3-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Instance Methods

### Behaviors and functions

**Instance methods** define what we can actually do with an object, their behaviors and functions.

These methods have direct access to the instance variables and can use them and change them to perform their tasks.

## Static vs Instance

### How methods are called

In Unit 1, we used and wrote static class methods like `Math.random()`, which were called with the classname.

Instance methods are not marked `static`.

They are always called using an object of the class and can access the object's instance variables.

## Method Location and Access

### Defined after variables and constructors

Methods are defined in the class after the instance variables and constructors.

They are usually marked as `public` so they can be accessed from inside or outside the class.

Methods designated as `private` are not accessible outside of the class.

They can only be used as helper methods by other methods inside the same class.

## Method Pattern

### Header and body

Methods define what the object can do.

They typically start with `public`, then a type, then the name of the method followed by parentheses for optional parameters.

Methods defined for an object can access and use its instance variables.

## Person `print` Method

### `void` method

The `Person` class has a `print` method that prints out all the data stored for a person object.

```java
public void print()
{
    System.out.println("Name: " + name);
    System.out.println("Email: " + email);
    System.out.println("Phone Number: " + phoneNumber);
}
```

## Method Header

### Return type, name, and parentheses

The method is marked as `public`.

After `public` comes the return type.

The `void` return type is used to indicate that the method does not return anything but has some effect, such as printing to the screen.

After the return type comes the method name followed by parentheses containing the list of parameters.

## Object-Specific Data

### `print` uses this object's values

The method can access and use the instance variables defined in the class:

::: {.tight-list}
- `name`
- `email`
- `phoneNumber`
:::

It gets the values specific to the object we called `print` on.

## Calling a Method

### Use an object and the dot operator

To call a method, we need an object that is an instance of the class, such as we get by calling its constructor.

Then we use the dot (`.`) operator to call its `public` methods.

```java
// call the constructor to create a new person
Person p1 = new Person("Sana", "sana@gmail.com", "123-456-7890");
// call p1's print method
p1.print();
```

## Check Your Understanding

### `clickablearea:: name_methods`

Click on all the lines of code that are part of a method in the following class.

```java
public class Name
{
    private String first;
    private String last;

    public Name(String theFirst, String theLast)
    {
        first = theFirst;
        last = theLast;
    }

    public void setFirst(String theFirst)
    {
        first = theFirst;
    }

    public void setLast(String theLast)
    {
        last = theLast;
    }
}
```

## Answer Reasoning

### Method lines

Correct method lines are the `setFirst` method and the `setLast` method:

```java
public void setFirst(String theFirst)
{
    first = theFirst;
}

public void setLast(String theLast)
{
    last = theLast;
}
```

The constructor `public Name(String theFirst, String theLast)` is not counted as a method in this check.

## Active Code

### `activecode:: class-add-print-method`

The following `Name` class keeps track of the first and last name of a person.

Add a method `print` that prints out the instance variables `first` and `last`.

Note that the methods have direct access and share the instance variables.

## Print Method Starter

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class Name
{
    private String first;
    private String last;

    public Name(String theFirst, String theLast)
    {
        first = theFirst;
        last = theLast;
    }

    // Complete the print method below to print out the
    // first and last instance variables with a space in between
    public void print()
    {

    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        Name n = new Name("Ada", "Lovelace");
        n.print();
    }
}
```
:::

## Test Targets

### Runestone checks

The Runestone tests check both `main` and the `print` method.

```text
Expected output from main:
Ada Lovelace

Expected output from print method with object Mickey Mouse:
Mickey Mouse
```

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- define instance methods as object behaviors and functions
- explain that instance methods can access and change instance variables
- distinguish instance methods from static class methods
- identify `public`, return type, method name, parameters, and body
- call an instance method using an object and the dot operator
- keep the starter `print` body blank until students complete it
:::

## End

### 3.3 Part 3 complete

Next: Virtual Pet and community class design.
